docs.title: Contributing to AetherSDR
docs.text: This page describes how to open the contributing guide, which covers submitting code, bug reports, and feature requests for AetherSDR.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be running.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Help in the menu bar.
  2. Click Contributing to AetherSDR....

The contributing guide opens. Follow the instructions there to submit code contributions, file bug reports, or propose feature requests.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• AetherSDR Help
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Enable Applet Panel
docs.text: Toggle the visibility of the applet panel, which runs along the right side of the main window.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be open.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Applet Panel to check or uncheck it.

When checked, the applet panel is visible. When unchecked, it is hidden.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Understanding the AetherSDR applet panel
  • Enable Pop Out Applet Panel
  • Reset Applet Order
  • Enable Minimal Mode

docs.title: Enable Autostart for CAT with AetherSDR
docs.text: Enabling autostart for CAT instructs AetherSDR to create virtual serial ports for CAT control each time the application launches, so external logging or contest software can connect without manual intervention.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running on Linux or macOS. This feature creates virtual serial ports and is not available on Windows.
  • Confirm that any software you intend to connect via CAT is not already holding the virtual ports open from a previous session.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Click Autostart CAT with AetherSDR.

The menu item is checkable. A check mark next to the label means autostart is enabled. Clicking the item again removes the check mark and disables autostart.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Persisted key Autostart CAT with AetherSDR Checkable menu item. When checked, AetherSDR creates virtual serial ports for CAT control on every launch (Linux and macOS only). AutoStartCAT

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• The setting takes effect on the next launch of AetherSDR. Toggling it during a running session does not immediately start or stop CAT; restart the application to apply the change.
  • If you also use rigctld for CAT control, see Enable autostart for rigctld with AetherSDR — running both simultaneously may cause port conflicts.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Virtual serial ports do not appear after enabling autostart — Verify you are running AetherSDR on Linux or macOS. This feature is not active on Windows. Also confirm you have restarted AetherSDR after enabling the setting.
  • CAT software cannot open the virtual port — Another process may already hold the port open. Close any other CAT clients, then restart AetherSDR.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable autostart for rigctld with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for TCI with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for DAX with AetherSDR
  • USB Cables

docs.title: Enable autostart for DAX with AetherSDR
docs.text: This page explains how to configure AetherSDR to start the DAX audio bridge automatically each time the application launches. Use this if you want DAX audio routing available immediately without enabling it manually each session.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be installed and able to connect to a FLEX-8600 radio.
  • DAX audio bridge support requires macOS or a Linux system with PipeWire.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Click Autostart DAX with AetherSDR.

The menu item is a checkmark toggle. When checked, AetherSDR will start the DAX audio bridge automatically on each launch. Click the item again to disable autostart.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Persisted key Autostart DAX with AetherSDR Checkable menu item. When checked, AetherSDR starts the DAX audio bridge on launch. Applies on macOS and PipeWire-based Linux systems. AutoStartDAX

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• The menu item has no effect on Windows — DAX autostart is supported on macOS and PipeWire-based Linux only. On other platforms, configure DAX audio routing through your system audio settings.
  • DAX audio bridge does not start despite the item being checked — Verify that PipeWire is running on your Linux system, or that your macOS audio subsystem is available at login. Uncheck and recheck Autostart DAX with AetherSDR to reset the setting.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable autostart for TCI with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for CAT with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for rigctld with AetherSDR
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Enable Autostart for rigctld with AetherSDR
docs.text: When this setting is enabled, AetherSDR automatically spawns a rigctld CAT server each time the application starts. This lets external logging, contest, and digital mode software connect to your radio via the standard rigctld interface without requiring manual startup steps.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be installed and able to reach your FLEX-8600 radio.
  • rigctld must be installed and accessible on your system PATH.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Click Autostart rigctld with AetherSDR.

The menu item toggles on or off each time you click it. A check mark next to the label indicates it is enabled. The setting persists across sessions via the AutoStartRigctld AppSettings key.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Persisted key Autostart rigctld with AetherSDR Checkable menu item. When checked, AetherSDR spawns rigctld at startup. When unchecked, rigctld is not started automatically. AutoStartRigctld

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• To disable autostart, click Settings > Autostart rigctld with AetherSDR again to remove the check mark.
  • If you only need virtual serial port CAT control rather than rigctld, see Enable autostart for CAT with AetherSDR.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• rigctld does not start on launch — Confirm that rigctld is installed and available on your system PATH. If the binary cannot be found, AetherSDR cannot spawn it regardless of this setting.
  • External software cannot connect to rigctld — Verify that the port rigctld listens on is not blocked by a firewall and matches the port configured in your external software. See your rigctld documentation for port configuration.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable autostart for CAT with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for TCI with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for DAX with AetherSDR
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Enable Autostart for TCI with AetherSDR
docs.text: This page explains how to configure AetherSDR to start the TCI server automatically each time the application launches, so you do not need to enable TCI manually after every restart.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be built with WebSocket support (HAVE_WEBSOCKETS). If the menu item is absent, your build does not include TCI.
  • You should be familiar with how TCI client software connects to AetherSDR's TCI server.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Click Autostart TCI with AetherSDR.

The menu item is a checkable toggle. A check mark next to the label means autostart is enabled. Clicking it again removes the check mark and disables autostart.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Default Persisted key Autostart TCI with AetherSDR When checked, AetherSDR starts the TCI server immediately on launch without requiring manual activation. Off AutoStartTCI

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• If you only run TCI clients some of the time, leave autostart off and start the TCI server manually when needed to avoid unnecessary resource use.
  • The setting is saved immediately when you toggle the menu item. No restart of AetherSDR is required for future launches to respect the new value.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable autostart for CAT with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for DAX with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for rigctld with AetherSDR
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Enable Blink Status Indicator
docs.text: View > Blink Status Indicator is a checkable menu item that controls whether the title bar status indicator blinks to signal the radio's heartbeat. Disabling it keeps the indicator static if the blinking is distracting.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running.
  • A FLEX-8600 radio does not need to be connected to toggle this setting, but the blinking behavior is only visible when a radio heartbeat is active.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Blink Status Indicator to toggle it.
  3. A check mark next to the item means blinking is enabled.
  4. No check mark means the indicator is static.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Default Description Blink Status Indicator On (checked) When enabled, the title bar status indicator blinks in response to the radio heartbeat signal. When disabled, the indicator remains static.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable Frameless Window
  • Enable Minimal Mode

docs.title: Enable Frameless Window
docs.text: View > Frameless Window toggles the custom frameless window style for the AetherSDR main window. When enabled, AetherSDR manages its own title bar, giving you a consistent look across desktop environments and precise control over window chrome.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running.
  • Frameless Window is on by default in v0.9.0. If you have upgraded from an earlier version, it is forced on once automatically.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Frameless Window. A checkmark next to the item indicates it is on.

Alternatively, press Ctrl+Shift+F to toggle the setting without opening the menu.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text:
Control Description Default Persisted key View > Frameless Window Toggles Qt::FramelessWindowHint on the main window. When on, the native OS window decorations are removed and replaced with AetherSDR's own 20px title bar. On FramelessWindow

When Frameless Window is on:

  • A 20px custom title bar appears at the top of the window. Drag it to move the window.
  • Double-click the title bar to maximize or restore the window.
  • Minimize, maximize, and close buttons appear in the title bar.
  • A resize grip appears in the bottom-right corner of the window.

When Frameless Window is off:

  • The native OS window decorations are restored.
  • The custom title bar and bottom-right resize grip are hidden.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• If your desktop environment or window manager conflicts with AetherSDR's custom title bar (for example, double title bars or missing window controls), turn Frameless Window off to fall back to native decorations.
  • The setting persists across restarts via FramelessWindow.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Two title bars visible — Your window manager is drawing its own title bar on top of AetherSDR's. Go to View > Frameless Window and confirm the checkmark is present. If the issue persists, check your window manager's settings for overriding client-side decorations.
  • Window cannot be moved or resized after turning Frameless Window off — The native title bar may not have appeared yet. Try minimizing and restoring the window so the window manager redraws its decorations.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Configure UI Scale
  • Enable Minimal Mode

docs.title: Enable Inhibit during TUNE
docs.text: Use Settings > Inhibit during TUNE to select which TX outputs the FLEX-8600 suppresses while the tuner is running. This prevents RF from reaching amplifiers or antenna ports that should not be keyed during a tune cycle.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be connected to a FLEX-8600 radio.
  • Confirm which TX output ports are in use at your station before changing these settings.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Hover over Inhibit during TUNE to open the submenu.
  3. Click any combination of the following checkboxes to enable inhibit on those outputs during tuning:
  4. None
  5. ACC TX
  6. TX1
  7. TX2
  8. TX3

A checkmark next to an item means that output will be suppressed during a tune cycle. Selecting None indicates no outputs are inhibited.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Checkbox Effect when checked None No TX outputs are suppressed during tuning. ACC TX Suppresses the ACC TX output during tuning. TX1 Suppresses the TX1 output during tuning. TX2 Suppresses the TX2 output during tuning. TX3 Suppresses the TX3 output during tuning.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• You can check multiple outputs simultaneously. For example, checking TX1 and TX2 inhibits both ports while leaving TX3 active.
  • Per-band inhibit settings are available in Settings > TX Band Settings..., which provides finer control over inhibit behavior by band.

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • Configuring AetherSDR Controls

docs.title: Enable Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Toggle keyboard shortcut processing on or off. When enabled, keyboard shortcuts — such as using arrow keys to adjust sliders — are active throughout the application.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be running and connected to a radio.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Keyboard Shortcuts to toggle it on or off. A check mark indicates shortcuts are enabled.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Default Keyboard Shortcuts Checkable menu item. When checked, keyboard shortcut processing is active (e.g. arrow keys on sliders). When unchecked, shortcuts are suppressed. See tips

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• If arrow keys or other keyboard shortcuts are interfering with text entry or other input, uncheck Keyboard Shortcuts to disable shortcut processing.
  • To change which keys are bound to which actions, use View > Configure Shortcuts....

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • Configuring AetherSDR Controls

docs.title: Enable Minimal Mode
docs.text: Minimal Mode hides the title bar and controls to give the panadapter the maximum available screen area. Use it when you want a clean, uncluttered view of the spectrum.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be running and connected to a FLEX-8600 radio.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Minimal Mode.

The item is checkable. A check mark next to Minimal Mode means it is active. Clicking it again removes the check mark and restores the title bar and controls.

You can also toggle Minimal Mode at any time with the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+M.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• If you also want to remove the window frame entirely, see Enable Frameless Window.
  • To recover hidden controls when the menu bar is not visible, press Ctrl+M to toggle Minimal Mode off.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable Frameless Window
  • Configure UI Scale
  • Enable Applet Panel

docs.title: Enable Pan Follows VFO
docs.text: View > Pan Follows VFO controls whether the panadapter automatically scrolls to keep the active VFO frequency visible when you tune outside the current view.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be connected to a FLEX-8600 radio so that the panadapter is active.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Pan Follows VFO to toggle the checkmark.
  3. Checkmark present: the panadapter pans automatically to keep the VFO in view.
  4. Checkmark absent: the panadapter stays fixed; the VFO can move outside the visible span without the view following.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Default Behavior Pan Follows VFO On (checked) When enabled, the panadapter scrolls whenever the VFO frequency moves outside the current display span. When disabled, the panadapter does not move regardless of VFO position.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: • If you prefer to scroll the panadapter manually and tune by clicking within the displayed span, disable Pan Follows VFO and enable View > Single-Click to Tune so clicks retune the VFO without moving the panadapter center.

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • Enable Single-Click to Tune

docs.title: Enable Pop Out Applet Panel
docs.text: Use View > Pop Out Applet Panel to detach the applet panel from the main window into its own floating window, or to dock it back into the main window.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • The applet panel must be visible. If it is hidden, enable it first via View > Applet Panel.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Pop Out Applet Panel. The applet panel detaches into a separate floating window. A checkmark appears next to the menu item when the panel is popped out.
  3. To dock the panel back into the main window, click View > Pop Out Applet Panel again.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Description Shortcut Default Pop Out Applet Panel Checkable. Floats the applet panel into a separate window when checked; docks it back into the main window when unchecked. Ctrl+Shift+S Docked (unchecked)

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• You can also toggle the pop-out state with the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+S without opening the menu.
  • Moving or resizing the floating window does not affect the main window layout.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable Applet Panel
  • Reset Applet Order
  • Understanding the AetherSDR applet panel

docs.title: Enable Propagation Conditions
docs.text: View > Propagation Conditions is a checkable menu item that toggles a real-time ionospheric propagation forecast overlay on your panadapters. Enable it to see current band condition data without leaving the main display.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running and connected to a FLEX-8600 radio.
  • Your system must have network access to retrieve propagation forecast data.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Propagation Conditions to check or uncheck it.

When checked, the propagation forecast overlay appears on your panadapters. Click the item again to remove the overlay.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • Configure Band Plan
  • Enable Pan Follows VFO

docs.title: Enable Single-Click to Tune
docs.text: This page explains how to enable single-click tuning on the panadapter. By default, AetherSDR requires a double-click to retune; enabling this option reduces that to a single click.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running and connected to a FLEX-8600 radio.
  • A panadapter must be visible in the main window.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Single-Click to Tune.

A checkmark appears next to the item when it is enabled. Click it again to return to double-click behavior.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: Control Default Behavior View > Single-Click to Tune Off (unchecked) When checked, a single click on the panadapter retunes the VFO to that frequency. When unchecked, a double-click is required.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: • If you find yourself accidentally retuning while reading the panadapter display, disable this option to restore the double-click requirement.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable Pan Follows VFO
  • Configure Band Plan

docs.title: FlexControl
docs.text: Settings > FlexControl... opens the serial port configuration for the FlexControl hardware interface, allowing you to assign and configure the serial port AetherSDR uses to communicate with a FlexControl knob controller.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• A FlexControl hardware device must be physically connected to your computer via serial or USB-serial adapter.
  • AetherSDR must have been built with serial port support (HAVE_SERIALPORT). If FlexControl... does not appear in the Settings menu, your build does not include this feature.
  • AetherSDR must be running.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings in the menu bar.
  2. Click FlexControl....
  3. In the dialog that opens, select the serial port your FlexControl device is connected to.
  4. Adjust any additional serial port parameters as needed.
  5. Click the confirmation control to apply the settings.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• USB Cables
  • Configuring AetherSDR Controls
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Help > Getting Started... opens the built-in beginner guide for connecting to and using AetherSDR with a FLEX-8600 radio.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR is installed and running on your system.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Help in the menu bar.
  2. Click Getting Started....

The beginner guide opens in a new window.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• AetherSDR Help
  • Configuring AetherSDR Controls
  • Understanding the AetherSDR applet panel

docs.title: Import/Export Profiles
docs.text: This page covers the Profiles > Import/Export Profiles... menu item, which is intended for backing up and sharing AetherSDR profiles.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • This feature is not yet implemented. The menu item exists but does not open a functional dialog.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Profiles > Import/Export Profiles....
  2. No dialog opens. The feature is not yet implemented.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • AetherSDR Help

docs.title: Quit
docs.text: Closes AetherSDR. Use this menu item when you are done operating and want to shut down the application cleanly.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • Save or export any profiles or settings you want to keep before quitting.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click File > Quit.

Alternatively, press Ctrl+Q.

docs.title: Related
docs.text: • Getting Started

docs.title: Reset Applet Order
docs.text: View > Reset Applet Order restores the applet panel to its default order. Use this if you have rearranged applets and want to return to the original layout.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • The applet panel must be visible. If it is hidden, enable it first via View > Applet Panel.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click View in the menu bar.
  2. Click Reset Applet Order.

The applet panel immediately returns to its default order. No confirmation dialog appears.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Enable Applet Panel
  • Enable Pop Out Applet Panel
  • Understanding the AetherSDR applet panel

docs.title: Understanding Noise Cancellation
docs.text: This page describes how to open the built-in noise cancellation guide in AetherSDR, which explains the NR2, NR4, DFNR, and MNR noise reduction algorithms available on the FLEX-8600.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text: • AetherSDR must be running.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Help in the menu bar.
  2. Click Understanding Noise Cancellation....

The guide opens and covers the NR2, NR4, DFNR, and MNR noise reduction modes.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• AetherSDR Help
  • Configuring AetherSDR Controls

docs.title: USB Cables
docs.text: Settings > USB Cables... opens the USB Cables tab of the Radio Setup dialog, where you assign USB cable connections for CAT control and audio routing.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be connected to a FLEX-8600 radio.
  • Any USB cables you want to assign must already be connected to the radio and recognized by it.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings > USB Cables....
  2. The Radio Setup dialog opens with the USB Cables tab active.
  3. Configure your USB cable assignments for CAT and audio as needed.
  4. Close the dialog when finished.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Getting Started
  • Enable autostart for CAT with AetherSDR
  • Enable autostart for DAX with AetherSDR
  • Configuring Data Modes

docs.title: Full AppSettings Key Reference
docs.text: This page lists every persisted AppSettings key in AetherSDR, the UI control or menu item that writes it, and where to find that control. Use it when troubleshooting configuration files, scripting deployments, or understanding what a setting does behind the menu label.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• No connection to a radio is required to read or understand these settings.
  • Settings are persisted locally on your machine. Changing a radio profile on the radio itself does not alter these keys.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text: The table below covers every key surfaced in the supplied catalog. Keys are sorted by the menu or dialog where they appear.

docs.title: Settings menu
docs.text: AppSettings key Menu item / control What it does Notes AutoStartRigctld Settings > Autostart rigctld with AetherSDR When checked, spawns the rigctld CAT server automatically each time AetherSDR launches. — AutoStartCAT Settings > Autostart CAT with AetherSDR When checked, creates virtual serial ports for CAT control on launch. Linux and macOS only. AutoStartTCI Settings > Autostart TCI with AetherSDR When checked, starts the TCI server automatically on launch. Requires WebSockets build. AutoStartDAX Settings > Autostart DAX with AetherSDR When checked, enables the DAX audio bridge on launch. macOS and PipeWire only.

docs.title: View menu
docs.text:
The View menu items listed below write their state as persisted preferences. Specific AppSettings key names for View items are not confirmed in the supplied catalog; do not assume key names for these controls. See the menu directly at View for their current state.

Control Menu item What it does Applet panel visibility View > Applet Panel Toggles the right-side applet panel on or off. Applet panel float View > Pop Out Applet Panel Floats the applet panel into a separate window or docks it back. Shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+S. Single-click tuning View > Single-Click to Tune When enabled, a single click on the panadapter retunes VFO. Default is double-click. Panadapter tracking View > Pan Follows VFO When enabled, the panadapter scrolls to keep the VFO visible. On by default. Minimal mode View > Minimal Mode Hides the title bar and controls to maximize panadapter area. Shortcut: Ctrl+M. Frameless window View > Frameless Window Hides OS window decoration. Shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+F. Propagation overlay View > Propagation Conditions Displays a real-time ionospheric propagation forecast overlay on panadapters. Keyboard shortcuts active View > Keyboard Shortcuts Enables or disables keyboard shortcut processing globally. Status indicator blink View > Blink Status Indicator Title bar indicator blinks to signal radio heartbeat. On by default. UI scale View > UI Scale Sets the application UI scale. Options: 75%, 85%, 100%, 110%, 125%, 150%, 175%, 200%. Band plan display View > Band Plan Controls band plan overlay size. Options: Off, Small (6 pt), Medium (10 pt), Large (12 pt), Huge (16 pt), plus region selector.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• The four AutoStart* keys are the most commonly scripted settings. They allow you to control which companion servers start with AetherSDR without opening any dialog.
  • AutoStartCAT applies only on Linux and macOS. On Windows, use AutoStartRigctld for CAT integration via rigctld instead.
  • AutoStartTCI is only present if AetherSDR was built with WebSocket support. If the menu item Settings > Autostart TCI with AetherSDR is absent, the key has no effect even if set manually.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• A companion server (rigctld, TCI, DAX) does not start at launch — Check that the corresponding AutoStart* key is set by verifying the checkmark is present in the Settings menu. If the menu item is missing entirely, your build of AetherSDR does not include support for that feature.
  • AutoStartCAT has no effect on Windows — Virtual serial port CAT is not supported on Windows. Use Settings > Autostart rigctld with AetherSDR (AutoStartRigctld) instead.
  • UI scale or panel layout resets after update — These preferences are stored locally. If the settings file is removed or corrupted, all View preferences return to defaults. Reconfigure them from the View menu.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Diagnose common problems: audio underruns, network jitter, slice configuration issues. For interactive diagnostics, see the in-app Slice Troubleshooting dialog.

docs.title: Diagnose audio underruns and jitter
docs.text: Use this page to read the audio buffer and jitter indicators in Network Diagnostics and identify why received audio sounds choppy, silent, or glitchy.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• AetherSDR must be running. The dialog does not require an active radio connection, but the audio indicators are only meaningful when a radio is connected and streaming audio.
  • Open the dialog and leave it visible while you reproduce the audio problem.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Settings > Network... to open the Network Diagnostics dialog.
  2. Locate the Audio Playback group in the lower-right area of the dialog.
  3. Watch RX Buffer Now and RX Buffer Peak. Values are shown in bytes and milliseconds. A buffer that stays near zero while underruns rise indicates the playback thread is starving.
  4. Watch Underruns (total) and Underruns (last sec). Underruns (last sec) resets each second, so a sustained non-zero value here means the problem is ongoing rather than historical.
  5. Watch Audio Arrival Gap and Max Arrival Gap. These show the inter-packet arrival timing. A large Max Arrival Gap relative to Audio Arrival Gap indicates bursts of late packets even if no packets were dropped.
  6. Watch Network Jitter. This is a smoothed estimate of timing variation in the audio stream. Rising jitter with rising underruns points to a network delivery problem rather than a local audio device problem.
  7. Cross-check the Packet Loss (Sequence Gaps) group. Find the Audio row under that group. A non-zero drop count confirms actual packet loss in addition to any jitter.
  8. Click Close when finished.

docs.title: What each control does
docs.text:
Indicator Meaning RX Buffer Now Current audio buffer fill, in bytes and milliseconds. RX Buffer Peak Highest buffer fill seen since the dialog was opened. Underruns (total) Cumulative count of audio buffer underruns since connect. Underruns (last sec) Underruns counted in the most recent one-second interval. Audio Arrival Gap Typical inter-packet arrival interval, in milliseconds. Max Arrival Gap Largest single inter-packet gap seen, in milliseconds. Network Jitter Smoothed jitter estimate for the audio stream, in milliseconds.

All indicators refresh every second.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Zero packet loss in the Audio drop row does not rule out jitter. Packets can arrive late in bursts without being dropped, and Max Arrival Gap will reflect that where the drop counter will not.
  • If Underruns (last sec) is zero but Underruns (total) is non-zero, the problem was transient and may have resolved on its own.
  • If RX Buffer Peak is high but RX Buffer Now is near zero and underruns are rising, the buffer filled briefly on connect and then drained, which can indicate the audio output device is running faster than the incoming stream rate.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Underruns rising, RX Buffer Now near zero, Network Jitter low — The network is delivering audio on time but the local audio output device may be consuming faster than packets arrive. Check your audio output sample rate and device settings in Settings > Radio Setup....
  • Underruns rising, Max Arrival Gap much larger than Audio Arrival Gap — Packets are arriving in bursts. The network path has bursty delay. Reduce other traffic on the local network or switch to a wired connection.
  • Audio drop count non-zero in Packet Loss group — Packets are being lost in transit, not just delayed. Check for wireless interference or switch network interfaces. See Measure RTT and packet drops during audio problems.
  • Network Jitter rising steadily — The network path is increasingly unstable. Check Latency (RTT) and Max Latency (RTT) in the Network Status group for corroborating evidence.
